channel.title: Nicotine Science and Policy
channel.description: Daily digest of views, debates and discussions on nicotine science, policy, regulation and advocacy.

item.title: Will Europe follow Sweden’s smoke-free playbook?
item.description: The EU debates regulating cigarettes versus non-combustible nicotine products, referencing Sweden's successful smoking decline. Smoke Free Sweden warns that taxing safer alternatives may reverse progress. MEP Weimers highlights Sweden's success with harm reduction policies. Experts advocate for harm reduction approaches and diversifying quit-smoking aids, especially for women. Policy discussions emphasize the importance of providing safer options and considering comparative risks. Attendees stress the need for consumer-centered regulation, echoing Sweden's successful outcomes. The event underscores the potential public health and economic benefits of embracing harm reduction strategies like those in Sweden. Calls are made to communicate risks clearly, regulate alternatives, and prioritize what works to reduce smoking rates across Europe. (Published on March 19, 2026bytheparliamentmagazine.eu)

item.title: Greece launches digital age verification system to curb tobacco and alcohol sales to minors
item.description: Greece has introduced a new digital system to verify the age of customers purchasing tobacco and alcohol, aiming to better enforce existing laws banning sales to minors. The initiative is part of a broader government effort to close loopholes that previously allowed retailers to avoid responsibility when selling to underage individuals. The system is integrated into the country’s digital infrastructure and is inspired by EU standards for online age verification, making it one of the first real-world implementations of this approach in Europe. Authorities say it will simplify inspections and make it easier to impose fines on those who break the law. Overall, the move reflects a growing trend of using digital tools to strengthen public health policies and protect minors. (Published on March 19, 2026byeuronews.com)

item.title: Australia to toughen tobacco crime laws as cigarette and vape black markets surge
item.description: Australia is introducing stricter laws to combat the rapidly growing black market for illegal cigarettes and vaping products. The government is responding to concerns that organised crime groups are increasingly profiting from illicit tobacco sales, which have become widespread across the country. New measures are expected to strengthen enforcement powers, increase penalties, and better target illegal supply chains and retailers involved in the trade. Authorities argue that existing regulations have not been sufficient to curb the problem, as demand for nicotine products continues to fuel underground markets. Overall, the reforms aim to reduce criminal activity, protect public health, and regain control over the legal tobacco and vaping market. (Published on March 19, 2026byabc.net.au)

item.title: Vaping Is Safer Than Smoking: Now It’s Official in France
item.description: France’s national health agency ANSES has concluded that vaping is significantly less harmful than smoking after reviewing a large body of scientific evidence. The analysis of numerous studies shows that, although not risk-free, e-cigarettes expose users to fewer toxic substances than traditional cigarettes. Researchers point out that potential risks—such as cardiovascular or respiratory effects—appear to be lower and are not always clearly established. The findings support a harm reduction approach, suggesting that switching to vaping may lower health risks for people who would otherwise continue smoking. At the same time, experts stress the importance of caution, especially to prevent uptake among young people and non-smokers. (Published on March 19, 2026byfiltermag.org)

item.title: Media Watch: More Propaganda Language Games About Nicotine Pouches
item.description: Tobacco control public health communication may manipulate headlines and lack evidence. A Health eNews article questioned the safety of nicotine pouches without proper support. The use of misleading headlines influences public perception, similar to past misconceptions about vaping. Misrepresenting the safety of nicotine pouches and tobacco products is prevalent. Dr. Milind Mehta raises concerns without substantial evidence, fueling fear unjustly. The comparison to smokeless tobacco products lacks relevance to modern alternatives like nicotine pouches. Such misinformation harms smokers seeking safer options. There is a clear need for accurate information in tobacco control discourse to guide consumers effectively and prevent false beliefs perpetuated by sensationalized claims. (Published on March 19, 2026bydaily-pouch.com)
